topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt cavernous, suddenly hollowed out. I stood by the mahogany desk, my fingers tracing the cool, polished wood, a nervous rhythm building beneath my skin. Professor Alistair’s chair remained stubbornly empty. It had been three weeks since his final project submission, three weeks of him being the unwavering North Star for my entire career trajectory. I checked the digital calendar again, a frantic sweep across the screen, searching for any sign of an announcement, any indication of his departure. Nothing. Just the lingering scent of old paper and strong coffee. A tightness constricted my chest; I needed a word, a confirmation, anything to anchor myself before the silence became too vast. I took a deep, shaky breath, the air tasting thin, and started walking toward the door, my steps quick and uneven.

[story 2]

I found him in the library archives, ostensibly looking for a specific monograph, but really just needing proximity. He was cataloging dusty volumes, his movements slow and deliberate, a stark contrast to the usual whirlwind of his presence. When I finally approached, my voice came out surprisingly high-pitched. "Professor? I just wanted to—" He looked up, his eyes holding a familiar, distant quality that made my stomach clench. There was no warmth in his gaze, only a practiced neutrality. "I'm not around anymore, Alex. I've stepped down." The words landed flatly, without the customary softening of farewell. I felt a sudden, sharp deflation, as if a tightly wound spring had simply snapped. My hand instinctively tightened into a fist at my side. I managed a nod, my throat suddenly dry, and turned to leave, the sound of my own footsteps echoing too loudly in the quiet room.

[story 3]

The news hit me like a physical blow during the staff meeting. Alistair hadn't been invited, hadn't even been mentioned in the preliminary correspondence. I sat ramrod straight, my knuckles white where I gripped my notepad. Every other face in the room seemed to move with a sluggish apathy, but my focus was laser-sharp, fixed on the empty chair at the head of the table where he usually presided. A strange, buzzing energy started to vibrate just behind my sternum, a restless need to fill the void he had left. I leaned forward, my posture radiating an almost desperate need for explanation. "Is everything alright?" I asked, my voice sounding too loud in the hushed room. Alistair hadn't looked up. He had simply adjusted his glasses, a small, almost imperceptible motion, and then the meeting dissolved into a strained silence that felt like a deliberate dismissal.
